metadata.summary: Celery 5.4+ with Django 5.x for async task execution: pass IDs not instances, idempotent tasks, delay_on_commit inside transactions, explicit retry policies and time limits.

content.title: Django Celery Tasks
content.summary: Celery 5.4+ with Django 5.x enables asynchronous task execution outside the HTTP request/response cycle. Tasks accept primitive arguments (IDs, not model instances), are idempotent by design, declare explicit retry policies (autoretry_for, retry_backoff), and set both soft_time_limit and time_limit. Inside @transaction.atomic blocks, always call task.delay_on_commit() — never task.delay() — to prevent the task from running before the DB commit completes.

why.p: Blocking operations (email, PDF, image processing, external API calls) inside a request degrade response latency, cannot be retried on failure, and exhaust WSGI workers. Celery decouples task execution from the request cycle, provides durable retry with backoff, and supports chain/group/chord primitives for multi-step workflows. The delay_on_commit fix alone eliminates an entire class of race conditions common in transactional views.

list.item: Any Django operation taking >100ms that is not critical to the HTTP response.
Scheduled batch work (nightly aggregations, reminder emails) via Celery Beat.
Retry-with-backoff for flaky third-party APIs (Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid).
Long-running background flows orchestrated as chain/chord/group.
CPU-intensive work (image processing, ML inference) that needs dedicated worker resources.
Sub-100/day jobs — python manage.py + system cron is simpler.
Simple async I/O inside a request — Django 4.1+ async views work.
Tasks that must succeed inline with the user response (payment authorization).
Tiny apps where running Redis + worker + beat is pure operational overhead.

rule.statement: Pass only JSON-serialisable primitives (IDs, strings, ints, dicts) as task arguments. Never pass ORM model instances.
rule.rationale: Model instances pickle, but on retry the instance may be stale or deleted. Fetch inside the task using the ID.

example.note: Pickle succeeds but retry fetches a potentially different state.

rule.statement: Every task must be idempotent. Use a DB timestamp flag (sent_at, processed_at) or a UUID idempotency key. Check the flag at task entry and return early if already done.
rule.rationale: acks_late=True + worker crash causes re-delivery. Without idempotency, the task runs twice.

rule.statement: Inside any @transaction.atomic block, call task.delay_on_commit() instead of task.delay(). Never call delay() directly inside an atomic block.
rule.rationale: delay() inside atomic can dispatch the task before the DB commit completes; the task then queries a row that does not yet exist.

rule.statement: Every task must declare both soft_time_limit (catchable SoftTimeLimitExceeded) and time_limit (hard kill). Catch SoftTimeLimitExceeded for graceful cleanup.
rule.rationale: Without limits, a stuck task holds a worker indefinitely. Soft gives cleanup time; hard ensures the worker is freed.

rule.statement: Declare retry exceptions explicitly via autoretry_for. Always use retry_backoff=True and retry_jitter=True. Set max_retries. Never use a bare except to swallow and not raise.
rule.rationale: Explicit retry exceptions prevent accidentally retrying unrecoverable errors (404, validation failure). Backoff + jitter prevents thundering-herd on broker recovery.

rule.statement: Define at least two queues: one for fast/critical tasks (orders, payments) and one for slow/background tasks (reports, emails). Pin worker concurrency per queue.
rule.rationale: A slow report task should not block payment processing sharing the same worker pool.

rule.statement: For long-running tasks, call self.update_state(state="PROGRESS", meta={current, total, percent}) every N items so Flower and polling clients can track progress.
rule.rationale: Without updates, Celery reports STARTED until completion; operators cannot tell if a task is running or stuck.

rule.statement: Use chain() for sequential steps where each step receives the previous result. Use chord(group, callback) when parallel tasks must aggregate into one result. Do not call .get() on a chord header inside a worker — it deadlocks prefork pools.
rule.rationale: chain and chord are serialisable workflow descriptions; .get() blocks the worker thread.

example.code:
from celery import chain, chord, group
# Sequential workflow workflow = chain( validate_order.s(order_id), reserve_inventory.s(), charge_payment.s(), send_confirmation.s(), ) result = workflow.apply_async()
# Parallel + aggregation report_workflow = chord( (calculate_user_metrics.s(uid) for uid in user_ids), aggregate_metrics.s() ) report_workflow.apply_async()

@shared_task( name="integrations.call_payment_gateway", bind=True, max_retries=3, autoretry_for=(requests.exceptions.Timeout, requests.exceptions.ConnectionError), retry_backoff=True, rate_limit="10/m", soft_time_limit=30, time_limit=45, ) def call_payment_gateway(self, payment_id: int, action: str) -> dict: response = requests.post( f"{settings.PAYMENT_GATEWAY_URL}/{action}", json={"payment_id": payment_id}, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {settings.PAYMENT_API_KEY}"}, timeout=25, ) if response.status_code == 429: raise self.retry(countdown=int(response.headers.get("Retry-After", 60))) response.raise_for_status() return {"status": "success", "data": response.json()}

antipattern.description: LLMs trained on pre-5.4 docs emit transaction.on_commit(lambda: task.delay(...)) boilerplate instead of task.delay_on_commit(...).
antipattern.reason: Both work, but delay_on_commit is cleaner. Standardise on the modern form. Document which version to use in the project AGENTS.md.

antipattern.description: Agents add bind=True to a task but never call self.retry, silently losing retry capability.
antipattern.reason: bind=True is required for self.retry calls and self.request introspection. If neither is needed, omit bind=True.

antipattern.description: Celery Beat in file-based scheduler mode resets schedule on every container redeploy; some periodic tasks miss their slot.
antipattern.reason: Use django-celery-beat (DatabaseScheduler) or celery-redbeat so schedules survive deploys.

antipattern.description: Agents use CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=True in tests and ship code that only works in eager mode.
antipattern.reason: Eager mode hides broker bugs (visibility timeout, serialiser mismatch). Add at least one integration test against a real Redis worker.

antipattern.description: Flower is wired behind a public ingress without authentication, exposing task payloads.
antipattern.reason: Always require basic-auth or VPN-only access for Flower. Task payloads may contain PII or credentials.

template.purpose: Canonical task skeleton: bind=True, autoretry, backoff, soft_time_limit, idempotency guard.
Full Django CELERY_* settings block for reliability in production.
Worker + beat + flower + Redis services for local/prod.
LLM prompt template for generating a complete idempotent task with tests.
